text: The patient is positioned supine on the operating table, with arms secured and legs placed in stirrups for optimal access during the laparoscopic hysterectomy procedure.
The surgeon positions the laparoscopic ports on the patient's abdomen, preparing for the hysterectomy procedure.
The surgeon positions the uterine manipulator within the pelvic cavity, ensuring proper alignment for the laparoscopic hysterectomy procedure.
The surgeon identifies the ureter within the pelvic space anatomy and uses a vessel sealer to manage surrounding vessels.
The surgeon performs dissection of the uterine attachments, carefully separating the uterus from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon approaches the window zone, preparing to continue dissection in the pelvic space during the hysterectomy procedure.
The surgeon mobilizes the bladder and dissects the osteoprotonium, carefully separating these structures from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon secures the uterine artery using a vessel sealer, ensuring hemostasis in the pelvic space.
The surgeon separates the cervix from the vagina, carefully dissecting the tissue to detach the cervix.
The surgeon performs a colpotomy to form the vaginal vault, creating an opening at the top of the vaginal canal.
The surgeon removes the uterus along with the adnexal tissue, completing the excision of these anatomical structures.
The surgeon sutures the vaginal vault, followed by suturing the port site, ensuring closure and securing the surgical area.

After manipulating the bowel out of the pelvis, the surgeon transsects the ligament and proceeds to the anterior leaflet of the broad ligament. The anterior peritoneal reflection of the uterus is opened to perform skeletonization in the grey zone, avoiding the intestinal lobe and ureter.

The surgeon uses atraumatic forceps to reflect the bladder anteriorly. The infundibulopelvic (IP) ligament is transected while ensuring the ureter remains secure. The uterus is then elevated and directed posteriorly.
The surgeon proceeds to mobilize the bladder, likely using atraumatic forceps to reflect it anteriorly, as part of the hysterectomy procedure.
The surgeon prepares to perform a transection, likely involving the uterine structures, following the elevation and posterior direction of the uterus.
The surgeon reflects the bladder anteriorly, ensuring it is positioned two centimeters below the colpotomizer in preparation for the vaginal vault closure.
The surgeon opens the colpotomy at the vaginal fornices using a monopolar hook or spatula, preparing to fix the colpotomy site.
The surgeon pulls the uterus into the vagina while dissecting the bladder by pushing the vesical fascia downward using scissors and a uterine manipulator.
The surgeon approaches the uterine artery, preparing for its identification and potential ligation or transection.
The surgeon performs a transection, likely involving the uterine structures, using surgical instruments.
The surgeon uses a bipolar electrosurgical instrument, visible in the surgical field, to perform cauterization or dissection.
The surgeon secures hemostasis by ligating the infundibulopelvic (IP) ligament on both sides, ensuring no bleeding occurs during the procedure.
The surgeon approaches the uterine vessel, entering the gray zone for skeletonization using the wand technique, ensuring all actions are performed under direct visual guidance.
The surgeon lifts the ovary and approaches the IP ligament during the uterus detachment, ensuring all steps are performed under direct vision, especially when using the ligature device.
The surgeon performs a precise transection and dissection using bipolar coagulation, achieving a bloodless field under direct vision.

The uterine manipulator is positioned protruding over the edge of the table, allowing for optimal movement. The surgeon skeletonizes the uterine artery, maintaining a seventy-degree working angle towards the pelvis.
The surgeon uses a bipolar instrument near the incision site, manipulating tissues with precision.
The video shows the positions of the surgical ports used for the hysterectomy procedure.
The surgeon begins making incisions to initiate the hysterectomy procedure.
The surgeon uses a monopolar hook to perform a colpotomy, making incisions around the vaginal fornices.
The surgeon uses a monopolar hook with a pure cutting waveform to perform precise cuts during the hysterectomy procedure.
The surgeon pulls the uterus into the vagina to maintain hemoprotein levels during suturing, ensuring it remains inflated. The surgeon's back is prepared and positioned inside a glove.

The surgeon begins dissection, highlighting the clean incision made with the monopolar hook. The colpotomizer cup is pushed up firmly to maintain its position on top, emphasizing its importance in the procedure.
The uterine manipulator is visible in the surgical field, positioned to assist in maneuvering the uterus during the hysterectomy procedure.
The surgeon completes the excision of the targeted tissue, removing it entirely from the surgical field.
The surgeon creates the vaginal fold during the hysterectomy procedure, manipulating the tissue to form a distinct fold.
The surgeon begins the extraction process, likely involving the removal of the uterus following its excision.
During the extraction phase, the surgeon handles a large uterus without using an endo knife, indicating the size of the uterus allows for manipulation without additional cutting instruments.
The surgeon uses a delayed absorbable suture, with the needle holder's arm length visible as they prepare for suturing.

The surgeon begins designing the vaginal fold, emphasizing the importance of the folds to prevent vault prolapse. The video shows the laparoscopic closure of the vaginal cuff, highlighting differences from transvaginal cuff closure.
The surgeon lifts the vaginal vault using sutures placed in the mid cuff and performs hemostasis repeatedly, ensuring control of bleeding. Copious irrigation is applied to the surgical area.
